nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, nutrient-rich, slow-moving or stagnant freshwater — ponds, swamps, rice paddies, and gently flowing streams across tropical and subtropical Asia

avoidWith: boisterous fish that uproot rosette plants

summary: Blyxa Japonica is a bushy, grass-like rosette plant prized in planted aquascapes for its soft texture and the golden-to-reddish highlights it tends to develop under strong light with adequate iron. Generally considered an intermediate plant, it performs best with medium-to-high light, CO2, and a nutrient-rich substrate.
careNotes: Blyxa Japonica is a true aquatic rosette plant in the family Hydrocharitaceae, valued in nature aquariums and iwagumi aquascapes for its soft, grass-like silhouette and the golden-to-reddish highlights it tends to develop under strong light with adequate iron. The trade name "Bamboo Plant" is sometimes applied to it in retail settings. Assessments of its difficulty vary — some hobbyist sources describe it as easy, others as demanding and finicky — and it is most commonly positioned as an intermediate plant not ideally suited to beginners. Lighting is commonly described as medium to high; roughly 30–40 µmol PAR is generally sufficient to keep it alive, around 100 µmol tends to promote a bronze hue, and around 200 µmol can drive deeper reddening. The substrate should be nutrient- and iron-rich — aquasoil or a clay-based mix is commonly recommended — as Blyxa Japonica is a heavy root feeder that benefits from root tabs alongside moderate water-column fertilization. When planting, trimming the lowest leaves to expose a short stem and tucking individual rosettes into the substrate tends to produce the best results. The plant is sensitive to algae buildup and generally dislikes unstable water conditions; drastic parameter swings have been reported to trigger melt and root rot, so keeping conditions steady and the tank well maintained is broadly advised. It is well suited as a bushy midground accent.
breedingNotes: Propagation is vegetative: mature plants produce lateral side shoots and small daughter plantlets at the base, which can be separated and replanted once they have developed their own roots. Stems can also be trimmed and replanted directly into nutrient-rich substrate. Seed reproduction is documented in the wild but is rarely pursued by aquarists.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Plant details page resolved from Tropica search for Blyxa japonica. Provided height (10–20+ cm), medium light demand (~0.5 W/L), medium CO2 (6–14 mg/L), medium growth rate, and Asian origin.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Hydrocharitaceae), height (4–8 in / ~10–20 cm), pH 6.5–7.5, midground/background placement, propagation by stem cuttings and runners, and noted that golden/red coloration develops under high light with CO2 and fertilization.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder
additional.notes: Used for native-range country list, taxonomy history, temperature (23–26 °C), pH 5.5–7.0, soft-water preference (gH 1–5), iron-rich substrate guidance, minimum tank size (~10 gal / 40 L), and the intermediate-to-difficult care assessment. pH range averaged with Buce Plant to 6.0–7.5.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomy confirmation: family Hydrocharitaceae, order Alismatales; alternate names 'Japanese blyxa' and 'dwarf Asian grass'.
